text: FLOHIMONT, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. and 12 m. SE of Givet, and com. FLO 319. It has some woollen manufactures.
FLOIFORS, a village of Sweden, in the prefecture of Œrebro, district and 14 m. NNW of Linde. There are copper-foundries here.
FLOING, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. and 3 m. NNW of Sedan. Pop. 1,312.
FLOIRAC, a village of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Carbon-Blanc, 3 m. ESE of Bordeaux. Pop. 1,200.
FLOMUNDA, a village of Turkey in Europe, in Upper Wallachia, to the SW of Gretschi, on the Danube.
FLONE, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, cant. of Jehay-Bodegnee. Pop. 119.

FLONHEIM, a market-town of the grand duchy of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of the Rhine, cant. and 5 m. NW of Alzey, and 18 m. SSW of Mayence, on the r. bank of the Wisbach. Pop. 1,602. It has 2 churches, and a synagogue. Fairs are held twice a-year. In the environs are quarries of excellent freestone.
FLOOD CREEK, a river of Central Australia, descending from Stanley's range, to the E of Mount Lyell, and probably finding its way to Lake Toren.
FLOOKBURGH, a chapelry, consisting of the township of Lower Holker, in the p. of Cartmel, Lancashire. Area 2,130 acres. Pop. 1,070.
FLOORE, a parish in Northamptonshire, 7 m. W of Northampton. Area 3,390 acres. Pop. 1,032.
FLOR, a village of Sweden, in the prefecture of Gefleborg, 11 m. W of Söderhomn, and 55 m. NNW of Gefle, on a small lake. It has some linen manufactures, and a paper-mill.

FLORAC, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Lozere. The arrond. comprises an area of 167,290 hectares, and contains 7 cant., viz.: Barre, Sainte-Enimie, Florac, Saint-Georges - de - Lévejac, Saint - Germain - de-Calberte, Meyrueis, and Pont-de-Montvert. Pop. in 1831, 41,525; in 1846, 40,780.—The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,717; in 1841, 8,762.—The town is 18 m. SSE of Mende, in a narrow but pleasant valley, on the l. bank of the Tarnon, a little above the confluence of that river with the Tarn. Pop. in 1789, 1,908; in 1821, 2,070; in 1831, 2,194; in 1841, 2,153. It consists of a single street and a square, and possesses but little trade. The culture of fruit forms the chief object of local industry.
FLORDON, a parish in Norfolk, 8 m. SW of Norwich. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 193.
FLORE'E, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and cant. of Namur. Pop. 462.

FLOREFFE, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and 6 m. WSW of Namur, and cant. of Fosse, on the r. bank of the Sambre. Pop. 1,423. An abbey was founded here by Godfrey, count of Namur, in 1121, which from its situation formed an important fortress. It was destroyed with the town in 1231.

FLORENCE, a compartimento or administrative prov. of Tuscany, comprising a portion of the Romagna territory, the upper part of the basin of the Siene or Mugello, a part of the Upper Valdarno, and all the basin of the Lower Arno to the confines of Pisa; and bounded on the N by the Papal prov. of Bologna; on the NE by that of Ravenna; on the E by the Tuscan prov. of Ravenna; on the S by that of Siena; on the W by that of Pisa, and the duchy of Lucca; and on the NW by the duchy of Modena. Its greatest length from E to W is about 60 m.; its breadth about 60 m. Its superficial area is estimated at 1,890 Italian sq. m. of 60 to a degree. Its pop. in 1836 was 671,857; in 1844, 721,723. It is divided into 28 cancellarie, which are subdivided into 90 comunitas; and contains 7 cities, viz., Firenze, Fiesole, Pescia, Pistoja, Prato, San-Miniato, and Volteria. A new administrative division of the grand duchy has recently been made, by which this prov. has been reduced in area to 1,489 sq. m., and its pop. to 585,899; but the details of this arrangement are not before us .- A railway has been completed from Florence to Leghorn; and another line has recently been opened between Florence and Pistoja. See Tuscany. FLORENCE, [ITAL. Firenze,] a celebrated city of Central Italy, the cap. of Tuscany, situated on both banks of the river Arno, at an alt. of 134 ft. above sea-level, nearly at the head of the broad and fertile vale which extends to Pisa, and thence to the sea, in N lat. 43° 46′ 41″, E long. 11° 15′ 55″; 150 m. NNW of Rome; 63 m. S by W of Bologna; and about 60 m. from the shores of that part of the Mediterranean called the Mare Inferum, or Tuscan sea. Few cities have received from nature more advantages than F. The Valdarno, or Vale of the Arno, is the Arcadia of the Italian poets. Behind the city rise hills covered with olive and fig-trees, and other plants natives of the warmer climates; still farther are high mountains, clothed with immense forests of chestnuttrees, and adorned with small towns, sometimes boldly rising on their sides, at other times half-concealed in their woods and recesses; while beyond these, in the distance, rise the naked and rugged summits of the lofty Apennines, to an alt. of 3,000 ft. above the plain. The whole valley is one continued grove and garden, where the beauty of the country is enlivened by the animation of the town, and the fertility of the soil redoubled by the industry of its cultivators. Such indeed is the richness of this plain, that five crops of wheat or maize are taken from it in the course of three years. The trees in the hedge-rows are covered with vines which are trained upon their stems; while the white villas that gleam through the orchards, and the populous hamlets that line the roads and the banks of the Arno, present on every side a scene of comfort and prosperity. The city itself spreads along the river, which forms one of its greatest ornaments, and intersects it from SE to NW. Its general outline is that of a square: three of its quarters are on the r., and the 4th on the l. bank of the river. An old wall about 5 m. in circuit, with 8 gates, surrounds it; and 5 bridges are thrown across the river. Its streets are well-paved or rather flagged, and wider than usual in southern climates; and its houses are in general solid and stately. It has several squares, and many churches and palaces; and though its streets are in some instances narrow, winding, and irregular, yet upon the whole it is a truly magnificent city,—"a city of bridges, churches, and palaces." [Bell.] Churches.] The first edifice which arrests the attention of the traveller is the cathedral,—a building of great extent and magnificence, in boldness and skill inferior only to St. Peter's at Rome. It is 491 ft. in length, and 363 ft. in height. It is completely cased with black, red, and white marble disposed in panels and foliage on the walls; and the interior is paved with variegated marble, part of which was arranged by Michael Angelo. Its most remarkable feature, however, is the dome, which was raised under the directions of Philip Brunelleschi, the most celebrated architect of the 15th cent. Its dimensions are within a few feet of the dome of St. Peter's; and as it is prior to it in date by nearly a cent., and was always the peculiar object of Michael Angelo's admiration, we may conclude that the plan of the Roman edifice was at least in part suggested by the Florentine. But the inferiority of the latter is undeniable. The octagonal shape, less simple, has consequently less grandeur than the circular; and, from being closed at the top, there is a want of light to illuminate the vast vault below. This is indeed the general defect of the church,-the windows being small, and the little light they admit diminished by the deep and rich colours of the painted glass. Such at least is the opinion of Italian critics, though to eyes accustomed to the gloomy grandeur of Gothic cathedrals these very defects may appear to be excellencies. The statues which adorn this church are most of them the works of the most eminent sculptors, and a few of the pictures are of the first class of excellence. Among the most remarkable are the statue of Brunelleschi, and those on the altar, the productions of Bandinelli and Michael Angelo. Of the paintings, those in the interior of the dome by Zuccheri and Vasari, and the portraits of Danté and Giotto, are most worthy of attention. Detached from the church stands the belfry,a light and elegant tower, 288 ft. in height, incrusted with variegated marble, and, like the church, adorned with statues. - The general baptistry of the city, which fronts the principal entry of the church, is also an octangular building of great magnificence. It is chiefly remarkable for the basso-relievos which adorn its three great bronze portals: they are the work of Andrea Ugalini of Pisa, and Lorenza Ghiberti, and were highly admired by Michael Angelo. Before the principal gate of the baptistry are two columns of porphyry, on which are suspended the immense chains with which the Pisans, in 1406, attempted to close up their harbour against the Florentines and Genoese, and which were afterwards brought to F. as a trophy of victory.—The next, and indeed the only other church which deserves particular description, is that of San Lorenzo, in the N part of the city. This also was planned by Brunelleschi, but is, both in design and magnificence, inferior to the Duomo. It has, however, attained to high celebrity from two buildings attached to it,— the sacristy and the Medicean chapel. The first was one of the earliest works of Michael Angelo, and is decorated with 7 statues by the same artist; and although most of them are unfinished, yet the eye of the connoisseur will easily discern in them the genius and boldness of design which so eminently characterize the productions of that great sculptor.— The chapel which adjoins the back of the church was begun in 1604 by Ferdinand I., Grand-duke of Tuscany, who intended not only to have removed thither the mausolea of his ancestors, but was in treaty to purchase the holy sepulchre at Jerusalem. "Its form is octagonal; its diam. 94 ft., and its elevation to the vault 200 ft. It is literally lined with lapis-lazuli, jasper, onyx, &c., furnished with sarcophagi of porphyry, and supported by granite pilasters with capitals of bronze. The niches between these pilasters are of touchstone; beneath is a subterraneous chapel, where the bodies, whose names are engraved on the sarcophagi above, are to repose. The crucifixion of our Saviour, a group in white marble by John of Bologna, with a Virgin by Michael Angelo, and St. John by one of his pupils, grace this dormitory of the dead, and preside over it with appropriate majesty. But, before the magnificent monument intended for their reception was finished, the Medicean line had failed; the work is now suspended; and, if we may judge from the impoverished state of the country, and the agitation of the times, It is not likely to be resumed for many years, if ever." -The Laurentian library, in the convent annexed to this church, has a collection of 120,000 vols. and above 6,000 valuable manuscripts, first formed by Cosmo and Lorenzo de Medici, and considerably increased by Leo X. and Clement VII.—In the other 1 4 5 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . The second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second s * - 4 1. churches of F., though not deficient in internal decoration, there is but little to interest the traveller, except the recollections which the tombs of the illustrious men whose ashes they contain are calculated to excite. It is indeed impossible to pass unnoticed the edifices wherein repose the remains of a Guicciardini or Machiavelli, of a Michael Angelo or Galileo. Palaces. The palaces of F. are remarkable for a style of architecture peculiar to themselves, to which the long civil wars in the 13th cent. between the Guelph and Ghibelline families first gave rise. The Palazzo Strozzi and the Palazzo Ricardi, the latter of which was built by the great Cosmo de Medici, are curious specimens of this style. They are square, heavy, solid masses, whose strength is their principal ornament. Their walls are thick, and broken by few windows, and these of a very diminutive size; and the basements are fortified with large unhewn masses of stone. The upper stories are faced with freestone; and the whole is crowned with a very heavy projecting cornice. In the palaces belonging to private persons, there are many pictures and statues by the best masters. Of these, the collections in the Ricardi and Gerini palaces are the most valuable. The Palazzo Vecchio, and Palazzo Pitti or Reale, the residences of the grand dukes were stripped of their pictures and statues by the French; but these have been restored to them.—But of all the collections of works of art which F. contains no one has acquired so high a reputation as the Medicean gallery. This magnificent building was erected by Cosmo I. in 1564; but the greater part of its contents were collected in the succeeding cent. by the cardinal Leopold de Medici, son of Cosmo II., and many additions were made to it by the princes of Lorraine and Austria. The busts of the Medicean princes and other contributors to the gallery adorn the vestibule. The gallery, or corridor, is in the shape of a Greek II, of which the two wings are each 430 ft. in length, and the intermediate part 97 ft. The paintings and statues in the gallery are arranged in a series of Florentine portraits, of illustrious foreigners, of painters, &c., and the busts of all the Roman emperors and their families, from Julius Cæsar to Constantine. The corridor is bordered on one side by a suite of halls or cabinets, each of which is consecrated to some set of masterpieces either in sculpture or painting, or collections of antique and modern medals, coins, gems, &c. The Venus de Medici, and the group of Niobe and her children, are generally considered models of the highest perfection. Under the same roof with the Royal gallery is the Magliavecchian library containing 150,000 vols. The mere enumeration of the various libraries, galleries, and museums of F. would exceed our limits; and we must refer our readers for further details to the Museum Florentinum, the Galleria di Firenze, and similar publications. Population.] In 1782, F. was calculated to contain about 97,000 inhabitants. The long period of misery and war which ensued greatly diminished its pop. In 1836, however, the pop. had remounted to 97,548; and in 1845, it was 106,899. "The Florentines," says a late native author, "are those among the Italians who most resemble their ancestors. We have in their language the same Attic salt which Boccacio diffused over his writings,—the same urbanity and taste even among the vulgar. The upper ranks of society at Florence are as refined as those of any other European capital; the desire of pleasing-what the French style prevenance-is one of their characteristics. The nobles have ceased to be idle and haughty; they now court the good opinion of their countrymen by forwarding and encouraging useful and liberal undertakings. The names of Ridolfi, Ginosi, Pucci, Capponi, Inghirami, and of other representatives of patrician families, are distinguished in the literary and scientific world. The activity of mind, the spirit of the old Florentine republicans, are not extinguished in their posterity, but have taken a more pacific direction towards agricultural industry, commercial speculations, and studies of every sort." Manufactures and Commerce.] As early as the 14th century, while the Venetians and Genoese were contending for superiority in the Levant, F. had become powerful, and its citizens wealthy, by their attention to commerce. As, however, they were not at that time possessed of a sea-port, their care and attention was principally directed to the improvement of their manufactures and objects of domestic industry. It appears from a contemporary historian that the silks and cloths of F. were its chief manufactures in the 14th cent. From the connections they acquired in various parts of Europe, and their individual wealth, the Florentines were naturally led to banking; and they soon became in this, as in their zeal for liberty, the successors of the citizens of the free states of Lombardy. The money trade of almost all Europe fell into their hands; and in several states, even the collection and administration of the public revenues were confided to their care. The immense fortunes acquired in these ways enabled the Florentines to enter into a still wider range of commercial exertion; and soon after the conquest of Pisa, we find Cosmo de Medici endeavouring to obtain for his countrymen a share in the Indian trade, then carried on by the Genoese and Venetians. In this he succeeded, by concluding a treaty with the sultan of Egypt which admitted the citizens of F. to all the privileges and immunities enjoyed by the Venetians in Alexandria. But the commercial greatness of F. fled with her liberty.—The present trade of F. consists entirely in the productions of her own territory, and her native manufactures, &c. Of the former, the chief are raw silks, oil, and wines; of the latter, the most celebrated are silk fabrics, which still continue to be esteemed, and are principally taffetas, damasks, velvets, and stuffs mixed with gold, silks, satins, and light stuffs commonly called F. taffetas. There are a variety of other manufactures conducted in F., among which straw-hats, artificial flowers, musical and scientific instruments, and fine porcelain have attained to high celebrity. An imitation of the Tuscan porcelain was introduced with great success into Britain by the late Mr. Wedgewood. History.] It is difficult to ascertain with certainty the precise era of the foundation of F. It appears to have been a place used for markets and fairs by the Etruscan inhabitants of the town of Fæsulæ,—now Fiesolé,—whose situation on one of the rocky eminences that command the Valdarno, was ill adapted for such purposes; and the first houses in F. were the booths erected for the accommodation of traders. Under the government of Sylla it became the seat of a Roman colony. The walls of the new city were first traced out by that dictator; and Florentia is supposed to have acquired its name from the officer who had the charge of the infant-colony. We find but little mention of it in history during the period that elapsed from the time of Sylla till the later ages of the Roman empire; but there can be no doubt, that, from the beauty of its situation, and its convenience for inland commerce, it must have greatly increased in population and riches. At the time that it was almost entirely destroyed by Totila, king of the Goths, F. abounded in baths, theatres, and aqueducts, a sure indication of the wealth and luxury of its inhabitants. During the iron sway of the Lombards in Italy it continued in ruins and obscurity until the end of the 8th cent., when it was rebuilt by Charlemagne, after that monarch had annihilated the Lombard government. Under the feeble princes of the Carlovingian family, and during the anarchy of the reigns of the dukes of Friuli, and the other petty sovereigns who successively assumed the title of king of Italy, and the subsequent disputes between the German emperors and the bishops of Rome, F., like the other cities of Tuscany and Lombardy, was gradually acquiring strength along with the spirit of liberty and independence. Exposed to sudden assaults of hordes of Saracens, Bulgarians, and other barbarous nations, against whom their powerless lords were unable to assist them, the first step towards freedom was the right granted to its citizens of surrounding their city with walls and fortifications. It was not, however, until the reign of Otho I., about the middle of the 10th cent, that the people were allowed to elect magistrates for themselves. The constitution adopted by the Florentines, was a deliberative council, or senate of 100 persons. For the execution of justice, consuls were chosen from among the most respectable class of citizens: of these there were originally 4, elected by the four quarters of the city. When it was divided into 6 districts, two new consuls were added, who were elected in the same manner. In 1207, the consuls were deprived of the right of judging in civil cases, and of pronouncing and executing criminal sentences, and these duties were assigned to a magistrate styled the Podesta. Although the Florentine government appears to have occasionally taken part in the quarrels between the emperors and the popes, and especially in that of Otho IV. and Innocent III., yet the internal peace of the state remained undisturbed. It was not until 1215, that a private quarrel having on a sudden kindled the spirit of party, the Florentines were engaged in a civil war which, after lasting 33 years with various success, at length terminated in the banishment of the Guelph or papal party, and forced the republic to take a decided part in the wars of Italy. Notwithstanding these long continued dissensions, however, the republic seems not to have suffered either in point of pop. or wealth. The annalists of that period talk of the necessary enlargement of the city, of the erection of public buildings, of the fortification of castles, and afford many other indications of its increase in strength and riches. It was, indeed, exclusively to such national purposes that the Florentines applied the fruits of their industry. Their manners and mode of living were simple to a degree, and all personal pomp and luxury were strictly repressed by sumptuary laws. The power of the Ghibelline, or imperial faction, was but of short duration in F. The citizens, harassed by the tyranny of the nobles, suddenly took up arms in the year 1250, and having deposed the Podesta, conferred his authority on an officer styled 'the captain of the people,' whom they associated with 12 magistrates called auziani, as councillors, 2 of whom were chosen by each quarter of the city. To this council they gave the name of 'the Seigniory,' and each of its members was to remain no longer in office than two months. The fortresses of the nobles were demolished, and the materials employed in repairing the walls of the city; and a public palace was built for the accommodation of the members of government. On the death of the emperor Frederic II. in 1251, the people seized the opportunity of recalling the banished faction of the Guelphs, and having forced the chiefs of both parties to sign a treaty of peace, they added to the captain of the people a Podesta of a Guelph family in Milan. No sooner was the popular government established in F. than the citizens, animated by past success, endeavoured to bring over the whole of Tuscany to their party. In 1258, the Ghibellines attempting to regain their ancient ascendancy, were, in their turn, expelled from F., and obliged to take refuge in Sienna, where they were not only received but protected, notwithstanding the threats and declaration of war by the Florentine Seigniory. The Ghibellines soon acquired a still more powerful protector, Manfred, king of Sicily, who at the solicitation of Farinata des Uberti, sent to their aid a small body of German cavalry. On the total defeat of these by the Florentines, Manfred sent 800 cavalry, besides infantry, into the state of Sienna. Alarmed at this new addition of strength to their enemies, the heads of the Guelphs hesitated to attack them in the Siennese territory; but the people, partly influenced by their distrust of the nobles, and partly by the intrigues of two of the auziani whom Uberti had gained over by bribery, insisted on invading Sienna. The Florentine army accordingly took the field, consisting of 3,000 cavalry and 30,000 infantry, and a battle was fought on the 4th Sept., 1260, at Monte-Aperto, on the banks of the Arbia, in which the Guelphs were completely defeated with the loss of 10,000 killed, besides an immense number of prisoners. The consequence of this defeat was a second expulsion of the principal Guelphs, who, with their families, were exiled from F., nine days after the battle. At a diet of the Ghibelline states of Tuscany, it was proposed to destroy completely the city of F., whose growing power and inclination to the opposite faction rendered it so dangerous to its neighbours; and this proposal meeting with the approbation of the Tuscan deputies, was nearly agreed on, had not the firmness and eloquence of Farinata des Uberti, whose abilities had so much contributed to his party's victory, been successful in altering the opinions of the diet, and preserving the independence of his country. For six years the Ghibellines retained the sovereignty, by the assistance of a garrison of the king of Sicily's soldiers under Count Guido Novella. The spirit of the people, however, was hostile, and the tyramical administration of Guido did not diminish their attachment to the pontifical faction. The defeat of Manfred at Grandella, by Charles of Anjou, and his subsequent death, raised the spirits of the exiles; and Count Guido, alarmed by an insurrection of the people, having deserted his post, the Guelphs, aided by 800 French under the command of Guy de Montfort, re-entered F. on Easter day 1267, and again expelled their ancient antagonists. The whole property of the Ghibellines was immediately confiscated; and, after the loss sustained by the Guelphs was repaired, thrown into a fund under the administration of particular magistrates, destined to provide for the constant maintenance and increase of the Guelph party. At this period, also, an important addition warrana strategy and a second FLO was made to the Florentine constitution of 4 new councils, without the co-operation of which, the seigniory could not determine on any point of importance. The first in order was 'the Council of the People,' consisting of 100 citizens. Next, but on the same day, the council 'De Credenza' was consulted; this consisted of 80 members, and in it the heads of the 7 principal trades had a right to a seat. From both these councils the watchful jealousy of the people excluded all nobles and Ghibellines. On the following day the business was considered by the council of the Podesta and the general council; the former composed of 90 members indiscriminately chosen from the nobles and plebeians, besides the heads of the trades; and the latter of 300 citizens of all denominations. The establishment of these councils tended greatly to increase the influence of the people. In 1279, by the zealous and active interposition of Cardinal Latino, legate and nephew to Pope Nicolas III., a peace was concluded between the Guelphs and Ghibellines; to the latter were restored their fortunes and estates, and the right of participation in the public offices. At the same time, the pious endeavours of the cardinal were successful in putting an end to a family feud between the Adimari and Donati, in which a considerable portion of the people had participated. In 1282, the Florentines adopted a new system of government, which continued until the downfall of the republic. This was a new seigniory, consisting of 6 members, who were styled 'priors of the arts and of liberty,' one being chosen from each of the incorporated professions, except that of the judges and notaries. These continued in office only two months; and, during that time, resided in the public palace, which they were not allowed to quit. The council of election consisted of the seigniory going out of office, with the heads and councils of the principal professions, and certain others chosen by them from the different quarters of the city. No person could be re-elected in less than two years, and a subsequent law excluded all nobles or gentlemen, although nominally members of the corporation, from being ever elected priors. This, and the other laws, tending to destroy the power of the nobles, were called the Ordinamenti della Giustizia, and were introduced by Giano della Bella, who, though a noble, had more regard for the general liberties of his country than the peculiar interests of his order. It was not the destiny of F. to continue long in a state of tranquillity. New factions arose in a few years; and the Guelphs, now divided into the two opposite parties of the Bianchi and Neri, combated among themselves with as much fury as when opposed to their former political antagonists. These wars were carried on with various success, until the Neri, assisted by Charles of Valois, succeeded in expelling the Bianchi, who were thus forced to join with the Ghibellines, their brethren in exile. The expedition of the emperor Henry of Luxemburg in 1310, revived the drooping spirits of the imperial faction; while, from the distinguished part F. held among his opponents, it gave to that republic a higher place in the scale of the Italian states than it had hitherto held. In fact, after this period, the history of F. is so much involved in that of the revolutions of Italy, that it becomes impossible even to narrate the changes of its internal policy, without entering largely into the general history of the times; and we must therefore close this article by referring our readers to the articles ITALY, TUSCANY, &c.

FLORENCE, a township of Oneida co., in the state of New York, U. S., 33 m. NW of Utica. It has an undulating surface, and is drained by tributaries of Fish river. The soil consists of sandy loam, and is well-adapted to pasture. Pop. in 1840, 1,259. -Also a township of St. Joseph co., in the state of Michigan, 144 m. WSW of Detroit. Pop. 528.— Also a village of Stewart co., in the state of Georgia, 153 m. SE of Milledgeville, on the E side of Chattahoochee river.—Also a village of Lauderdale co., in the state of Alabama, 135 m. NW of Tuscaloosa, advantageously situated on the N side of Tennessee river, between Muscle Shoals and Cypress creek, at an alt. of 100 ft. above the level of the river. The streets are 100 ft. in width, and cross each other at right angles. Pop. 2,000.—Also a village of Boone co., in the state of Kentucky, 76 m. N of Frankfort, 5 m. S of Ohio river. Pop. 100.—Also a township of Erie co., in the state of Ohio, 112 m. NE of Columbus. It contains extensive beds of iron-ore. Pop. 1,635.—Also a township of Williams co., in the same state. Pop. 119.—Also a village of Louisa co., in the state of Iowa, on the river of that name, which is here crossed by a ferry.—Also a village of Morgan co., in the state of Missouri, on the S fork of La Mine river.
FLORENCE (SAINT), a parish of Pembrokeshire. Pop. 396.

FLORENNES, a town of Belgium, capital of a cant. in the prov. of Namur, arrond. and 6 m. NE of Philippeville, on the r. bank of the Yves. Pop. 1,435. It has an ancient castle, and possesses extensive iron-works.
FLORENS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Gard, cant. of Saint Ambroix. Pop. 1,437.

FLORENSAC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Herault, arrond. of Beziers. -The cant. comprises 4 com. Pop. in 1831, 6,502; in 1841, 6,340.—The town is 15 m. ENE of Beziers, near the l. bank of the Herault. Pop. 3,454.

FLORENT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Cher, cant. of Charost. Pop. 1,195 .- Also a canton, commune, and fortified town of the island of Corsica, arrond. of Bastia. The cant. comprises 4 com. Pop. in 1831, 1,697; in 1841, 1,879. The town is 9 m. WSW of Bastia, on a gulf of the same name, near the mouth of the Aliso. Pop. 391. The adjacent marshes render its situation extremely unhealthy. The gulf of St. F., which is about 9 m. in depth, and 6 m. in medium breadth, forms a safe and commodious roadstead for vessels of the largest size. St. F. was founded in 1440, and has been repeatedly taken by the French and Genevese. Its fortifications were erected by the Corsicans in 1745, and in 1793 it made a long although ultimately unsuccessful resistance to the English. In 1783, the town was set on fire by lightning, and to a great extent destroyed.
FLORENT-DES-BOIS (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vendée, cant. of Bourbon- Vendée. Pop. 1,135.

FLORENT-LE-VIEIL (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Maineet-Loire, arrond. of Beaupreau.—The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 15,621; in 1841, 15,958.—The town, called during the Bonaparte republic Montglone, is 13 m. N of Beaupreau, on the I. bank of the Loire. Pop. 2,102. Fairs are held here twice a-year. The Vendean war commenced here on the 10th March 1793.
FLORENTIN, a fortress of Turkey in Europe, in Bulgaria, in the sanj. and 9 m. N of Widin, on the r. bank of the Danube.
FLORENTIN, a province of the grand duchy of Tuscany. See FLORENCE.

FLORENTIN (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, arrond. of Auxerre. — The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 6,448; in 1841, 6,181.—The town, formerly called Chateaudun, and during the Bonaparte republic Mont-Armance, is 17 m. NNE of Auxerre, on a hill at the confluence of the Armance and Armaçon, and near the fine aqueduct of the Burgundy canal, which crosses the Armance. Pop. in 1841, 2,407. It has several tanneries, and possesses a considerable trade in corn, timber, hemp, and charcoal. Fairs are held 7 times a-year.
FLORENTIN-LA-CAPELLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Saint- Amans. Pop. 1,157.

FLORENVILLE, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, arrond. and 12 m. SSW of Neufchateau, near the l. bank of the Semoy. Pop. 1,683. It has extensive breweries, numerous tanneries, and several flour, tan, and wire-mills.

FLORES, an island of the Azores group, about 30 m. in length, with a mean breadth of 9 m. The foot of Santa-Cruz is in N lat. 39° 27' 3", W long. 31° 8′ 37." [Vidal.] Sir Home Popham inferred the long. of Ponta-del-Gada, the N point of the island, to be 31° 11'. It has two towns, both on its E side, Santa-Cruz and Lagens, and four small villages. The entire pop. is about 15,000. The surface is mountainous, especially towards the S, with a prevailing slope to the E; but the soil is well-watered FLO and cultivated. The chief productions are wheat, pulse, lint, wine, cattle, sheep, hogs, and orchilla. This island is occasionally resorted to by homewardbound ships for refreshment and provisions; but has no regular foreign trade.
FLORES, an island of the North Pacific, near the W coast of Vancouver's island, in N lat. 49° 20', and W long. 126°. It is 36 m. in length, and 8 m. in medium breadth.

FLORES, a comarca and small town of Brazil, in the prov. of Pernambuco, 300 m. WSW of Olinda, at the head of the Rio-Pajehu, an affluent of the São-Francisco, and near the Serra Araripe. Pop. of district, 1,800. The culture of cotton and rearing of cattle form the chief objects of local industry.—Also a town and parish in the prov. of Goyaz, comarca and 60 m. SE of Cavalcante, near the r. bank of the Rio-Paranan. Pop. 2,400. Cattle are extensively reared in the environs.—Also a small town in the prov. of Ceara, 15 m. from São-João-do-Principe.

FLORES, a small island in the mouth of Rio-dela-Plata, 18 leagues W 3 S from Lobos, and 16 m. E of the port of Monte-Video. It has a lighthouse in S lat. 34° 56', W long. 55° 55', with a revolving light elevated 478 ft. above high water.
FLORES-DA'VILA, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 30 m. NNW of Avila, partido of Arevalo. Pop. 375.
FLORESTI, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Upper Wallachia, district of Vlaska, 27 m. W of Bucharest, near the l. bank of the Arjish. It has a Greek convent.

FLORIAN, a town of Austria, in the prov. of Upper Austria, circle of the Traun, 7 m. WSW of Ens, and 12 m. NNW of Steyer, on a hill near the Danube. It has a rich Augustine convent, with a fine collection of medals, a mineralogical museum, and one of the finest organs in Germany.—Also a village in the circle of the Inn, SE of Scharding.— Also a town of Styria, in the circle and 27 m. NW of Marburg, on the Lasnitz. Pop. 390.

FLORIDA, a territory of the United States; bounded on the N by Alabama and Georgia; on the E by the Atlantic; on the S and W by the gulf of Mexico; and on the W by the river Perdido. It extends about 400 m. from N to S, and has an average breadth of 140 m. It is situated between 25° and 31° of N lat., and between 80° 25' and 87° 20' W long. Its area is estimated at 52,268 sq. m. East F. consists of a large peninsula, extending 380 m. from the St. Mary river on the N, to Cape Sable on the S; and separating the gulf of Mexico from the Atlantic ocean, and a tract of land extending from the mouth of the St. Mary, W to the river Apalachicola. West F. extends along the N coast of the Mexican gulf, from the Appalachicola to the Perdido. It formerly extended to lakes Ponchartrain and Maurepas; thence along the river Ibberville to the Mississippi, and thence to the parallel of 31° N, from which a line drawn E to the Appalachicola formed its northern boundary. The part lying between the Mississippi and Pearl is now included in Louisiana; and the district between Pearl river and the Perdido, in the states of Mississippi and Alabama. The sea coast of both sections of F. is low, and the country continues very flat for more than 40 m. inland, when it becomes a little hilly and interspersed with rocks. Towards the W the rise is more gradual, and the surface more woody; but the elevation of the ridges between the river basins does not exceed 250 or 300 ft. The coast along the gulf is deeply indented with small gulfs and inlets, and intersected with numerous rivers; and several islands are included in the prov. which are of little importance or utility. The most remarkable are Santa-Rosa, a long and narrow sandy slip parallel to the coast, between Rose bay and that I of Pensacola; St. George, forming the SE side of Appalachicola bay; and the Tortugas, or F. kays, opposite the most S point of E. Florida, covered with mangrove bushes, and extending in an immense line from NE to SW. Some of these islands are mere sand, and none of them produce any thing better than grass and pines. The principal island on the E coast is Anastasia, opposite to the town of St. Augustine. It is divided from the mainland by a narrow channel, and is 25 m. in length. To the N of Anastasia is the fertile island of Amelia. There are a few good harbours on the coast, such as Pensacola and Tampa on the gulf, and St. Augustine and St. Mary's on the Atlantic. Rivers.] The rivers which empty themselves into the Atlantic ocean, are St. Mary's, the common boundary between Florida and Georgia, about 1 m. broad at its mouth; St. John's river, which, rising in a marshy tract near the middle of the peninsula, and flowing gently northward, traverses several lakes,the lowest of which, Lake George, is 20 m. long and 12 m. broad,—and falls into the sea about 40 m. S of the St. Mary, after a course of about 300 m. of which 200 are navigable for vessels of 6 ft. draft of water; and Indian river, which runs from N to S, and falls into the sea about 60 m. S of Cape Canaveral. Most of the rivers which fall into the gulf of Mexico have their source in the prov. of Georgia. The most worthy of notice are the Appalachicola, which divides East from West F., and is composed of two streams rising in the Apalachian mountains, and uniting on the confines of F.; the Escambia, which empties itself into the bay of Pensacola; and the Perdido, formerly the boundary between Louisiana and F., now that with Alabama, falling into the sea 4 leagues W of Pensacola bay. The other rivers are the Suwanee, Oscilla, Withlacoochee, Ockloconnee, and Tolopchopko.—Lakes are numerous, and some of them are large; that of Macaco, between the parallels of 26° and 27°, is the largest. Michasuckee lake, 15 m. NE of Tallabarra, is 12 m. long. General aspect and soil.] Florida in its general aspect is flat, sandy, and barren on the sea-shore; but advancing inland it becomes marshy, and abounds in natural meadows. It presents a rich and fertile appearance especially on the banks of the rivers; and for the space of 30 or 40 m. from the coast, there is scarcely to be seen a single stone weighing more than 2 or 3 lbs. The interior parts are more hilly and covered with wood; but the surface in these districts is stony, and the smaller size of the trees indicates decreasing fertility in the soil. The soil is commonly a white sand, lying on a bed of white clay; in some places a dark grey, brown, or black loam, on a foundation of whitish marl, chalk, and testaceous limestone. In the plains of West F. the vegetable mould is often perfectly black, soapy, and rich, lying on a deep bed of chalk or shells. A range of low hills extends through the peninsula. Climate. The climate of F. has been distinguished into two tracts, viz.—the N, which includes the continental and W parts of F., -and the S, which comprehends almost the whole of the peninsula. In the latter district, the therm. ranges in summer between 84° and 88° in the shade; and during the months of July and August frequently rises to 94°. At Fort Brooke, on Tampa bay, the mean temp. of winter is 64° 76'; and of summer 81° 25'. It very rarely freezes; nor is the cold ever so severe as to injure even the China orange-tree, the fruit of which is said to be here remarkably delicious. The air is pure and free from fogs, but the dews are generally excessive. In January the weather is wet and July and August thick, hot, and suffocating; but from the end of September to the end of June it is altogether inconceivably delightful. At this season, says Volney, "there is not perhaps a finer climate in the world." The E side of the peninsula is hotter than the W; and towards the S point of the W coast there are from May to August frequent squalls and tornadoes, but not of long continuance. On the E or Atlantic side the E trade-wind prevails; but on the W, or towards the gulf of Mexico, there are cooling sea-breezes from the NW throughout the whole season of summer. About the autumnal equinox, and during two or three months following, violent storms and dreadful hurricanes frequently occur. In the N climate the winds are colder and more variable, the frosts in winter more severe, the temp. more changeable, and fogs more prevalent; yet the town of St. Augustine, where this dampness is very great, but where there are no adjoining marshes, is the healthiest place throughout these latitudes, and many persons from the Havannah resort thither as to a Montpelier. In both tracts of climate, however, particularly in the N, there are great variations from heat to cold, amounting often to a change of 30° in the space of 12 hours. From the end of June to the middle of October-the season of the heavy rains, combined with violent heat-fevers are common, and are observed to be most obstinate near the rice and indigo plantations. Persons who sleep in the open air are very subject to tetanus; and intemperate habits are found to be utterly ruinous to health. Vegetable productions.] The vegetable productions of F. are particularly deserving of notice, both for their variety and luxuriant growth. Nothing can exceed the majestic appearance of its towering forest-trees, and the brilliant colours of its flowering shrubs. Pines, palms, cedars, and chestnuts, grow to an extraordinary height and size. Laurels, especially the magnolias, are striking objects, often rising with erect trunks to the height of 100 ft., forming towards the head a perfect cone, and having their dark green foliage silvered over with large milk-white flowers frequently 8 or 9 inches in diam. The live oak, after forming a trunk from 10 to 20 ft. high, and from 12 to 18 ft. in circumf., spreads its branches 50 paces on every side. The cypress, generally growing in watery places, has large roots like buttresses rising around its lower extremity, thence, rearing a stem of 80 or 90 ft., it throws out a flat horizontal top like an umbrella, so that, growing in forests of an equal height, they not unfrequently present the appearance of a green canopy supported upon columns in the air. The dog-wood trees rise to the height of 12 ft., and then spread their branches horizontally, which, meeting and interweaving with others on every side, form a shady grove, so dense and humid as completely to exclude the rays of the sun, and to suppress the growth of any other vegetable, thus presenting to the traveller a natural and sheltered arcade, sometimes extending for the space of 10 m. without interruption. But the most beautiful of the forest tribe is the tapering Carica papaya, which rises to the height of 20 ft., with a stem perfectly straight, smooth, and silver-coloured, having a spherical top of leaves always green, and ornamented at once with flowers and fruits.—Many rich fruits, particularly limes, prunes, peaches, and figs, grow wild in the forests; and grape-vines, whose stems are often 10 or 12 inches in diam., climb around the trunks of the trees to their tops; but those which produce the best fruit creep along close to the ground from one low shrub to another. Among the shrubs may be particularly mentioned a species of myrica called the wax-tree, which grows to the height of 9 stormy; in February and March dry and clear; in or 10 ft., and produces a number of large round berries, covered with a coat of white wax, which is | formed by the inhabitants into candles harder and more lasting than those made of bees'-wax.-Of the numerous flowering plants, we can only particularize a species of hybiscus, which, though a herbaceous plant, renewing its stem every year, yet grows to the height of 10 or 12 ft., branching regularly in the form of a sharp cone, and covered with hundreds of large expanded crimson flowers, which blow in succession during the whole of the summer and autumn months. That singular vegetable production generally called the long moss, which is found in most countries within the tropics, is remarkably prevalent in the woods of F. It grows from the limbs and twigs of all the trees, and pushing out on all sides its pendant branches, sometimes fills up as with a curtain the spaces between the arms of large trees, and at other times waves from the lower limbs like streams, to the length of 15 or 20 ft. When fresh it is eaten by cattle and deer in the winter season, and when properly dried it is found to answer better than any other substance for stuffing mattresses, chairs, saddles, &c. When steeped, dried, and beaten like hemp, it leaves a hard, black, elastic filament resembling horse hair, which the Spaniards in S. America work into cables that are said to be very strong and durable. In the marshes and banks of the lakes and rivers, the reeds and rushes grow to an extraordinary size, some of the former being actually 30 or 40 ft. high, and being used as masts to the canoes. Among the aquatic plants the Pistia stratiotes is particularly worthy of notice. It resembles a well-grown stock of garden lettuce, and vegetates from seed on the surface of stagnant water; but, associating in large quantities, it forms floating islands, which, though at first produced close to the shore, are sometimes carried by the wind or current into the river, where they are nourished by long fibrous roots descending to the muddy bottom, and then extend themselves like a green plain, several miles in length, and 1 m. in breadth, furnishing a habitation to crocodiles, frogs, otters, herons, curlews, &c.—The principal vegetable productions regularly cultivated for the subsistence of the inhabitants are corn, pulse, beans, potatoes, pompions, melons, rice, and a variety of esculent roots, particularly a species of arum, which is much cultivated in the maritime districts, and has a large turnip-like root resembling, when boiled or roasted, the taste of the yam. The produce of Indian corn in 1848 was 27,000,000 bushels; of wheat, 2,100,000 b.; of potatoes, 2,000,000 b. Much fine timber, pitch, tar, and turpentine, are obtained from the forests. Tobacco, cotton, and indigo, are raised in considerable quantities. The last mentioned article, made in East F. is accounted equal to the best Spanish produce. Minerals.] Among the mineral productions of F. are several kinds of precious stones, amethysts, turquoises, and lapis-lazuli. Ochre, pit-coal, and especially rich iron ore, are abundant. Animal kingdom.] Of domestic animals, F. possessed in 1840, 12,043 horses and mules, 118,081 horned cattle, 7,198 sheep, and 92,680 pigs.—The country is rich in its zoology, without producing many animals that are very formidable either from their ferocity or strength. Squirrels of several species, racoons, and opossums, are numerous: herds of deer and horned cattle, large and fat, but subject to extensive ulcerations in their thighs and haunches, supposed to be occasioned by their standing during the heats of summer in the lakes and rivers, to feed on the water-grass, are found in the interior; and horses running wild, as well as kept in herds, of a small breed, and almost as slender in their form as the American roebuck. Of the wilder tribes there | light clay colour, and not easily distinguished from a are the weasel, polecat, and lynx: the last a fierce little creature, preying upon young pigs, fawns, and turkeys; foxes of the small red species, which bark during the night, but move so precipitately that they are seldom heard twice in the same spot; wolves of different colours, and larger than a dog, occur in companies; and bears, in considerable numbers and of great strength.—Of birds, besides many which are migratory, there are found in F. vultures, hawks, rooks, jays, parrots, wood-peckers, pigeons, turkeys, herons, cranes, curlews, cormorants, pelicans, plovers, &c. A few of the more remarkable are the snake birds, a species of cormorant of great beauty, which delight to sit in peaceable communities on the dry limbs of trees, hanging over the lakes with their wings and tail expanded as if cooling themselves in the air, and when alarmed, drop as if dead into the water, suddenly appearing again on the surface at a great distance from the spot where they first sunk, but showing only their long slender head and neck above the water, which gives them very much the appearance of a snake. Among other birds are the crying bird, a species of pelican, about the size of a large domestic hen, and of a speckled colour; the wood-pelican, a large bird, nearly 3 ft. high when standing erect, and supposed to approach the nearest to the Egyptian ibis; the painted vulture, of a white or cream colour; and the great savannah crane, a very stately bird, about 16 ft. in length from the toes to the extremity of the beak when extended, and nearly 5 ft. when standing erect, and 8 or 9 ft. between the extremities of the expanded wings. — The coasts, sounds, and inlets, abound in excellent fish; and the inland lakes and rivers are, in some places, actually crowded with the finny tribe. Of these, the more remarkable are, the great brown spotted garr, from 5 to 6 ft. in length when fully grown, whose impenetrable skin resembles a coat of mail, and of which the scales are so sharp and strong that the Indians use them as points to their arrows; the mud fish, about 2 ft. long, the flesh of which is white and tender, but rather soft, and not much esteemed; the golden bream, or sun-fish, about 8 inches in length, resembling the trout in shape, remarkably strong and rapacious for his size, and delicious as food; the silver or white bream, and the black or blue bream, both which are also extremely beautiful and abundant; the cat-fish, sting-ray, scale-flounder, spotted bass, sheep's-head, drum, &c., and many other varieties. This multitude of fishes furnishes subsistence to an equally numerous brood of alligators, which are seen in all the rivers and lakes, in numerous troops, and many of them more than 20 ft. in length. Among the lizards of F., of which there are several species, are the little green chameleon, about 7 inches long, and very harmless; the striped lizard, called scorpions by the Americans; and a small blue lizard, remarkably swift, with a long slender tail, which is subject to be broken off like that of the glass snake. There are several kinds of tortoises in the rivers and lakes, of which the most deserving of notice is the great soft-shelled tortoise, some of which, when fullgrown, weigh from 30 to 40 lbs., and resembling very much in form and appearance the sea-turtle. The whole back-shell, except the vertebra and ribs, is cartilaginous, and easily reduced to a jelly when boiled. These creatures bury themselves in the slushy bottoms of rivers and ponds, under the roots of aquatic plants, leaving an aperture just sufficient for the head to play through, for the purpose of seizing their prey, which they do with great rapidity, and frequently drag even the young water-fowl from the surface. There is also the great land-tortoise, called gopher, found only on the dry sand-hills, of a stone. Of frogs there are various kinds, the largest of which is about 8 or 9 inches in length from the nose to the extremity of the toes, and has a loud hideous voice, inferior indeed to the bull-frog of Virginia, but greatly resembling the grunt of a hog. There are also red and black toads, the former of which are very large, weighing upwards of a pound, but no way venomous.-The snakes of F. are numerous, but little different from those which are found in the other southern provs. of the United States. The largest is the rattle-snake, which is commonly from 4 to 6 ft. in length, but sometimes even 8 or 10. The moccasin snake, which abounds in East F., is as large as the rattle-snake, and is said to be more formidable by being more apt to bite.- Incredible numbers of the small insects called ephemeræ, cover the surface of the lakes and rivers, supplying abundant food for the birds, frogs, and fishes; needle or a spark of fire on the skin. Gnats and musquitoes also are extremely frequent and troublesome, especially on the sea-coast, and in the rice and indigo plantations; but they are said to disappear in proportion as the land is cultivated. There are said to be no bees in West F.; but in the East province they are sufficiently numerous. Population.] When F. came into the possession of Great Britain, great encouragement was given to settlers, and considerable additions were made to its European inhabitants; but their increase was extremely slow, notwithstanding the salubrity of the climate, and the advantages offered by Government to the planters. The pop. in 1830 was returned at 34,730, of whom 15,510 were slaves; in 1840, at 54,107, of whom 25,559 were slaves; and in 1850, at 58,200, of whom 22,000 were slaves.—A few Seminoles, and other small Indian tribes, are scattered about in the forests and savanas.—There are found, particularly in East F., numerous monuments of apparent antiquity, with the nature of which none of the present Indians seem to have much acquaintance; and which, therefore, render it highly probable that the country was formerly occupied by a people more civilized and skilled in the arts of life than the Yamasees, Creeks, or any of the modern American tribes known to Europeans. These are pyramidal hills or artificial mounts, erected so as to command an extensive prospect of the adjacent country, and conjectured to have been intended as watch-towers, or places for sacrifice; vast tetragon terraces adjoining to these mounts, supposed to have been the foundations of fortresses; oblong sunken areas surrounded by a bank, sometimes by two rising one behind the other, resembling amphitheatres for the exhibitions of games or shows, and generally appropriated by the Indians of the present day to the execution of their captives; and artificial lakes or ponds to which spacious avenues run from the mounts. These ancient remains were seen by Bartram on the E shore of St. John's river, at the entrance of the great Lake George, on the opposite shore, on the bank of the Little Lake, on Dunn's island a little below Charlotteville, and on the W banks of the Musquito river, near New Smyrna. The Seminoles, who now inhabit these countries, are a mere handful of people, but having refused to emigrate to lands assigned to them beyond the Mississippi, have carried on a tedious and expensive war with the United States. They find in the spontaneous productions forests afford, a superfluity of subsistence; and secured in the midst of their swamps from any sudden attack, they have little to occasion disquietude, except the encroachments of the white people. Government.] This territory is governed by a General assembly, divided into two houses, a senate of 11 members chosen biennially, and a house-of-representatives composed of 29 members who are chosen annually, and a governor appointed by the authority of the President, with a salary of 1,500 dollars. The judges are elected by concurrent vote of both houses of assembly. The council meets annually at Tallahassee. The territory sends I delegate to Congress. It is divided into 4 districts, and 20 counties, viz.:- Pop. in Pop. in Leon, The territorial debt in 1840 amounted to 3,900,000 dollars. The revenue during the year ending Nov. 1, 1848, was 56,832 d.; the expenditure, 59,259 d.— The General assembly of 1848-49 chartered three companies for the establishment of railroads in this territory, viz., one to connect the Atlantic coast with that of the gulf of Mexico, passing across the higher part of the peninsula; another to communicate between the Chattahoochee river and St. Andrew's bay on the gulf of Mexico; and another to construct a road from some point on St. Mary's river to Pensacola. None of these roads have yet been commenced; but a small railroad runs from Tallahassee the cap. to its port, St. Mark's, a distance of 22 m.; and another from Lake Wicomico to St. Joseph, a distance of 12 m.—This territory has no college; but in 1840 had 18 academies with 732 students, and 51 primary schools with 925 scholars. History.] This country was first visited in 1497, by Sebastian Cabot, a Venetian mariner in the service of Henry of England; but was more completely explored by Juan Ponce de Leon, a native of Spain, in 1512. Discovering land on Palm-sunday, he gave it the name of F., from the Spanish name of that festival, Pasqua de Flores; or, according to Herrera, from the appearance of the country, which was covered with flowers, and the most beautiful blossoms. He landed on the coast; and, taking possession of the soil in name of his Catholic Majesty, erected a stone with an inscription commemorating his visit. But, having been prevented by the opposition of the natives from effecting a settlement, he returned to Puerto Rico. Another voyage was undertaken in 1528, by Pamphilo de Narvaez, who sailed with 400 men from the island of Cuba; and, attempting to penetrate into the interior of the country, was never more heard of. At length, in 1539, F. was entirely subdued by Ferdinand de Soto, one of the bravest officers in the Spanish service; but it cost the Spaniards a long and bloody struggle before they were able to establish themselves in the country. In 1564, the French began to form some small settlements on the coast. The Spaniards sent a fleet against their colony, and put all the settlers to the sword. A Frenchman named De Gourges made severe reprisals. in 1597; demolishing the forts erected by the Spaniards; but from this period the French neglected America, and the Spaniards. continued to make petty establishments on the coasts of F. In 1586, their principal fort, St. Augustine, was attacked by Sir Francis Drake, who reduced and pillaged the place; and, in 1665, it was entered and plundered by Captain Davis, at the head of a body of Buccaneers. In 1702, Colonel More, governor of Carolina, attempting to annex the country to the British dominions, marched a corps of 500 English and 700 Indians to the walls of St. Augustine, and besieged it for the space of three months; but the Spaniards, having sent a squadron to the relief of the garrison, he made a precipitate retreat, when on the point of accomplishing his enterprise. When a British colony was settled in Georgia, in 1733, the Spaniards became apprehensive of a new attempt upon F. In 1740 an expedition was fitted out against St. Augustine by General Oglethorpe; but the Spanish commander having received intelligence of the intended attack, made such additions to the strength of the garrison, and adopted such of the soil, and in the abundance of game which the skilful measures of defence, that the English were compelled after sustaining considerable loss to abandon their design. In 1763, F. was ceded to Great Britain in exchange for the Havannah, which had been taken from Spain; and numbers of colonists poured into the country from the neighbouring provs., the British islets, and all the Protestant nations of Europe. In 1781, F. was again recovered by the Spaniards, and was guaranteed to them by treaty in 1783. In 1810, a revolution took place in W. Florida; but its leaders were divided in opinion whether they should maintain their independence as a separate state, or accede to the American confederation. They sent agents to Washington, who had an interview with the President of the United States; and in the mean time, the American government—which had been prosecuting a claim upon the prov. since 1804-took measures for occupying the W division. The claim advanced by the American government arose out of a dispute respecting the boundaries of Louisiana, which Spain had ceded to France in 1801, and which the latter power sold soon after to the United States for the sum of 15,000,000 dollars. It was contended by the American government, that W. Florida formed a part of Louisiana; which they had purchased from France; and, on the ground of certain spoliations, alleged to have been committed by Spain on American commerce, they advanced pretensions upon E. Florida also, by way of indemnification. These pretensions were finally supported by actual invasion; and the whole prov. was ultimately ceded to the United States by treaty in October 1820.

FLORIDA, a township in Berkshire co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 7 m. E of Adams. Pop. 441.—Also a v. in Warwick township, in Orange co., New York. Pop. 250.—Also a township in Montgomery co., New York, 34 m. NNW of Albany. Pop. 5,214.—Also a township in Hillsdale co., in Michigan, 94 m. SW of Detroit. Pop. 297 .- Also a v. in Monroe co., in Missouri, 83 m. NE of Jefferson.
FLORIDA (CAPE), a promontory on the SE coast of Florida, forming the SW end of Biscayunekay, at the entrance of Hawk's channel, in N lat. 25° 41′ 50″, W long. 80° 3′ 0″.

FLORIDA (GULF OF), a name sometimes given to the channel between the peninsula of Florida and the Bahama islands, N of the island of Cuba, and through which the Gulf-stream finds a passage, and runs to the NE along the American coast. See GULF-STREAM.

FLORIDA (LA), one of the Solomon islands, in the S. Pacific ocean, discovered by Mendana. FLORIDA KAYS, TORTUGAS, OF MARTYR'S Islands, a chain of islets, rocks, and sand banks, extending from the S extremity of the peninsula of E. Florida inward, to the gulf of Mexico, between the parallels of 24° and 25°. They are supposed to have, at some former period, constituted 5 or 6 large islands, which have been gradually broken down by the operation of the Gulf-stream. There are 8 openings through the reef into the Gulf-stream, with not less than 18 ft. water in each. The high islands are based upon hard rock, and covered in some places with sand, in others with a bluish marl, on which manchineel, aloes, iron-wood, lignum-vitæ, cotton, and a great variety of trees flourish. The low islands, founded on coral rocks, are covered with a rich but wet soil. None of the islands, except Kay West, on which the United States have an arsenal, are inhabited. On Bush or Garden Kay, in N lat. 24° 36′ 0″, W long. 82° 52′ 35″, is a lighthouse. Immense quantities of fish and turtle swarm in the surrounding seas.
FLORIDA (Rio), a river of Mexico, in the state of Durango, which takes its rise in N lat. 26° 50', and after a N course of about 150 m., discharges itself into the Conchos, on the r. bank.
FLORIDIA, a town of Sicily, in the cant., and 7 m. W of Syracuse. Pop. 4,800.
FLORIDSDORF, a town of Lower Austria, 4 m. N of Vienna, on the railway to Olmutz. Pop, 1,060.
FLORIMONT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. and 4 m. E of Delle, on the l. bank of the Cauval. Pop. 450.
FLORINA, a village of Turkey in Europe, in Romelia, in the sanj. and 18 m. SE of Monastir. FLO
FLORINAS, a village of Sardinia, in the prov. and 10 m. SSE of Sassari. Pop. 1,440.

FLORIS, or Flores, a large island in the Indian archipelago, situated to the E of Java, between 120° and 123° E long., and 8° and 9° S lat. It is about 200 m. long from E to W, and has an average breadth of 36 m. The interior is very imperfectly The inland parts are mountainous and known. woody; and it contains several active volcanoes of considerable height. Towards the coast, the country is fine and open. The principal place frequented by English ships passing through the straits of F., is the village of Larantuka, near the E end, in S lat. 8° 45'. Endé, on the S side, has a good harbour, the only one indeed on the S side of the chain of islands, which extends from Java to Timor. Refreshments for two or three ships, such as goats, hogs, fowls, and fruits, buffaloes, and some turtle, and water, can be obtained here. In return for these, the natives receive gunpowder, musket balls, glass bottles, wine glasses, white linen cloth, and all kinds of coarse cutlery. Benzoin, ambergris, small quantities of bees' wax, horses, and slaves, are exported from the island, and sandal-wood in small quantities may also be obtained. Many of the natives profess Christianity, and they generally hoist the Portuguese flag; but in 1812 the Bugis expelled all the European settlers. They trade with Sandalwood island and with Singapore. The Timuri, a dark woolly-haired race, commence here, and extend over all the islands to the E, as far as Timor-Laut. The Burman language is said to prevail over the greatest part of the island.—The Portuguese visited this island at a very early period, and gave it the name of Floris; but there is no reason to believe that they ever established a regular settlement.
FLORISSANT, a village of St. Louis co., on the Missouri, 131 m. E of Jefferson.
FLORSHEIM, a town of the duchy of Nassau, on the Maine, 18 m. SE of Nassau. Pop. 2,000. It has manufactories of porcelain and pottery ware.
FLORSHEIM (OBER), a village of Hesse-Darmstadt, 7 m. WNW of Worms. Pop. 1,050.

FLOS, a township in Simcoe district of Upper Canada, intersected on the SW by Nottawasaga river. A lake of about 500 acres in extent is situated in its NW part, about 2 m. from Nottawasaga bay. Pop. in 1842, 200.
FLOSION, a lake of Sweden, in the NE of Jemtland, 27 m. in length from NW to SE, and 4 m. in greatest breadth.
FLOSS, a river of Silesia, which runs into the Oder 6 m. NW of Breslau.—Also a town of Bavaria, 5 m. ENE of Weiden.
FLOSSGRABEN, a river of Prussian Saxony, which rises 9 m. SSE of Weisenfells, and after a course of about 30 from S to N, joins the Luppe, 3 m. E of Merseburg.

FLOTA, or FLOTAY, one of the Orkney islands, situated between the islands of Faray and S. Ronaldshay. It is 3 m. long, and 2 m. broad. It is low in surface, and is entirely composed of sandstone, and sandstone-flag. Its heaths afford excellent sheeppasture, and abound with moor-fowl. F., with the small adjoining islands of Faray, Cava, and Gransey, contained, in 1841, 405 inhabitants. It is united with the parish of Walls in Hoy. This islet was the residence of the historiographer appointed by the crown of Norway to collect information with regard to the N of Scotland. These narrations formed a work called Codex Flotticensis; to which Torfæus is indebted for much of his history of the northern parts of Scotland.
FLOTTA, one of the smaller Hebrides, off the NW coast of Lewis.
FLOTTE (LA), a commune and town of France, on the N coast of the isle of Ré, 9 m. WNW of La Rochelle. Pop. 2,227. It has a good harbour.
FLOTZ, a small town of European Turkey, in Wallachia, near the confluence of the Jalonitza and the Danube.

FLOUR (SAINT), an arrondissement, canton, and town of France, in the dep. of Cantal.—The arrond. comprises the 5 cant. of Chaudesaigues, Saint F., Massiac, Pierrefort, and Ruines. Area 172,033 hect. Pop. in 1841, 61,690.—The cant. comprises 26 com. Pop. in 1841, 25,642.—The town, 30 m. E of Aurillac, on a basaltic rock rising from the r. bank of the Auzon, had a pop. of 6,422 in 1789; 6,464, in 1831; and 5,930 in 1841. It is the see of a bishop, suffragan of Bourges; and has a communal college, and a public library. It has a considerable suburb stretching along the banks of the Arder. A large quantity of orseille or archil, is collected on the neighbouring mountains. St. F. was anciently a strong fortress.
FLOURENS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Toulouse. Pop. 339.
FLOVIER (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Pressigny-le-Grand. Pop. 1,129.
FLOWERFIELD, a township in St. Joseph co., Michigan, U. S., 154 m. SW of Detroit. Pop. 281.
FLOWTON, a parish in Suffolk, 6 m. WNW of Ipswich. Area 800 acres. Pop. 179.

FLOYD, a county in the S part of Virginia, U.S., watered by Little river and its branches. Area 525 sq. m. Pop. in 1841, 4,453. The cap. is Jacksonville.—Also a co. in the NW part of Georgia, watered by the Oostanaula and Etawah, which unite in this co. to form the Coosa. Area 460 sq. m. Pop. 4,441. The cap. is Rome.—Also a co. in the E part of Kentucky, watered by Big Sandy river, and Kentucky and Licking rivers. Pop. 6,302. The cap. is Prestonburg.—Also a co. in the SE part of Iowa, skirting the Ohio on the NW. Area 144 sq. m. Pop. 9,454. The cap. is New Albany. - Also a township in Oneida co., in the state of New York, 8 m. N of Utica. Pop. 1,742.—Also a township in Putnam co., in the state of Iowa. Pop. 1,296.
FLOYDSBURG, a village in Oldham co., in the state of Kentucky, 37 m. W by S of Frankfort, on Floyd's creek, a branch of Salt river.
FLOYON, a village of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. and 6 m. SSW of Avesnes. Pop. 1,394.
FLUELEN, or Fluhlen, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Uri, on the E side of the lake of Uri, 2 m. N of Altorf.
FLUMARI, a town of Naples, in Principato- Ultra, 6 m. SSE of Ariano. Pop. 1,620.

FLUMENDOSA, a river of Sardinia, in the div. of Sassari, which rises 3 m. W of Lanusei, and after a course of about 50 m. from N to S and SE, flows into the Mediterranean by three small embouchures, a little to the N of Sarrabus. Its affluents are the Esterzili and the Perdas-de-Fogu. It is the Saphus of Ptolemy.
FLUMET, a small town of Savoy, on the Arly, 33 m. SE of Geneva. Pop. 1,000.

FLUMINI-MAJORI, a village near the W coast of Sardinia, in the prov. and 10 m. NW of Iglesias, in a gorge surrounded by mountains, and irrigated by a river of the same name. Pop. 1,455. There are hot springs here. The orange and lemon groves in the vicinity are remarkably productive of beautiful fruit; the sugar cane has also been cultivated with success.—The stream, after a course of 15 m., empties itself into the sea near Cape Pecora.
FLUMS, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of St. Gall, on the Seetz, 5 m. W of Sargans. Pop. 2,634. FOB
FLUORN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Schwarzwald, 3 m. W of Overndorf. Pop. 1,167.

FLURRY, a stream of cos. Armagh and Louth, which rises in the Slievegullion mountains, and runs S into the bay of Dundalk, about 13 m. NW of Dundalk. FLUSHING, Dutch, VLIESSINGEN, an important seaport of Holland, in the island of Walcheren, on the N side of the W. Scheldt, at the embouchure of that river, in N lat. 21° 26' 42", E long. 3° 24' 57". Pop. in 1840, 7,725. The approach to the harbour is between two jetties, which break the action of the sea. Inside of the town are two basins, one of such size and depth as to contain a fleet of men-of-war: hence the importance of this place to the French, who stipulated in 1795 for its possession jointly with the Dutch, and afterwards obtained it exclusively. The batteries sweep the mouth of the Scheldt, but the width of the river-3 miles-prevents them having the complete command of it. F. was a noted resort of English smugglers. The town is well built, and was the birth place of De-Ruyter. It sustained in 1809 a siege from the British army in the expedition against Antwerp; and was taken, but evacuated on 23d December in that year. Bonaparte soon after annexed the island of Walcheren to France.- Old F. is a suburb on the W side of the town.

FLUSHING, a township in Queen's co., in the state of New York, U.S., 9 m. E by N of New York, at the head of a bay of the same name. Pop. 4,124. About 2 m. N of the town is the Episcopal seminary of St. Paul's college.—Also a township in Belmont co., in Ohio, 110 m. E by N of Columbus. Pop. 1,683.—Also a township in Genessee co., in Michigan. Pop. 473.

FLUTED CAPE, a lofty promontory at the S entrance of Adventure bay, on the E coast of Bruny's island, in S lat. 43° 22'. It consists of immense basaltic columns of a reddish colour, rising 400 or 500 ft. above the level of the sea.

FLUVANNA, a county in the E of Virginia, U. S., skirted on the S by James river, which divides it from Buckingham. Area 416 sq. m. Pop. 8,812.—The cap. is Columbia.—Also a river of Virginia, which rises in the Blue mountains, and joins the Ravenna at Columbia, forming with it James river.

FLUVIA, a river of Spain, in Catalonia, which rises 7 m. SSW of Olot, passes Castel-follit, Besalu, and Bascara, and, after a course from W to E of about 50 m., empties itself into the Mediterranean near Ampurias.

FLY ISLAND, an island in the S. Pacific, in S lat. 15°, W long. 150° 20', discovered by Le Maire and Schouten in 1616, and so named by them from the number of flies seen there. It is covered with trees.
FLYFORD-GRAFTON, a parish of Worcestershire, 7 m. E of Worcester. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. in 1831, 242; in 1841, 229.
FLYOSE, a town of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. and 4 m. W of Draguignan. Pop. 2,833.
FLYSSERED, a village of Sweden, 36 m. NNW of Calmar, on the r. bank of the Æmma.

FNIOSKA, a stream watering a beautiful valley of the same name in the district of Hals, on the N coast of Iceland. The mountains on both sides of the valley of this stream are almost entirely covered with grass; and about 150 years ago, it exhibited one of the finest forests in Iceland, the remains of which are still visible on the E side of the river, in the numerous stumps of birch-trees which present themselves, some of them exceeding 2 ft. in diam.
FOBBING, a parish of Essex, 4 m. E of Hordonon-the-Hill. Area 1,830 acres. Pop. in 1831, 391; in 1841, 428.
FOBE', a village of Senegambia, in Futa-diallon, 42 m. N of Labbe.
FOCA, a small African island, at the mouth of the Calabar, with a town of the same name, called Wyndorp by the Dutch. Wood, water, and provisions may be obtained here.
FOCARO, a mountain of Naples, in the Terra d'Otranto, 20 m. NE of Tarento. Alt. 1,920 ft. above sea-level.

FOCHABERS, a town of Morayshire, in the p. of Bellie, 9 m. E of Elgin, situated on the E side of the river Spey, which is here crossed by a bridge 340 ft. long, and consisting of 4 arches. Pop. 1,135.

FO (Monte-DI), a steep mountain of the Tuscan Apennines, about 2 m. from Pietra-Mala. It is composed of slate-clay resting upon limestone. Near its base is a space of from 12 to 14 yds. in circumf., covered with loose earth and stones, from which lambent flames of carbonated hydrogen are constantly issuing, in twelve or more places at once. Williams has given a view of this phenomenon in the 1st vol. of his Travels in Italy, &c.

FO-SHAN, or Fun-shan, a town of China, in the prov. of Kwang-tung, in N lat. 23° 5', E long. 112° 45', situated on the banks of the river whereon Canton stands, and up to which the tide flows. It extends about 3 m. along the river, in a single row of large well-built houses, intersected in some places by lanes; and forms the general emporium for the Canton foreign trade.
FOCHAS, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, 145 m. NW of Jeddo.
FOCHEA. See Foggia in Asia Minor.
FOCKERBY, a township in the p. of Adlington, W. R. of Yorkshire, 9 m. SE of Howdon. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. in 1831, 103; in 1841, 92.

FOCZANI, a town of European Turkey, in Lower Wallachia, 54 m. WNW of Galatz, on the Milkow, which divides it into two parts, the one in Wallachia, the other in Moldavia. It was formerly large, and had a considerable trade, but was ruined in the military operations of 1789; and although rebuilt, has not recovered its former prosperity. The Turks were defeated here in 1789 by the Russians.
FODDERTY, a parish of Ross and Cromarty, chiefly situated in a valley surrounded with high hills. Pop. in 1811, 1,900; in 1841, 2,437.

FODIANDAMALE, the most elevated summit of the W. Ghauts, in the territory of Codugu or Kurg, 13 m. W of Pettah. It detaches itself in a picturesque manner from the range, and shoots up to an alt. of 5,682 ft. above sea-level.
FOE-PETUNE-HOTUN, a town of Chinese Tartary, 16 m. N of Petune-Hotun.
FOECY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cher, cant. of Mehun-sur-Yevres. Pop. 771.
FOEIL (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of St. Quentin, 9 m. SW of St. Brieuc. Pop. 2,485.
FOELDWAR, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Tolna, on the Danube, opposite Colocza.
FOGARAS. See FAGARAS.
FOGAU, a village of Styria, in the circle of Gratz, near the small town of Strass. It has a large and elegant church.
FOGELN, a small island on the W side of the gulf of Bothnia, in N lat. 60° 38'.
FOGGARA, a district in Abyssinia, consisting of a small strip, 35 m, in length and 12 m, in breadth, between the mountains of Begemdir and Lake Dembea.
FOGGATHORPE, a township in the p. of Bubwith, E. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. in 1831, 128; in 1841, 96.
FOGGI, a town on the W coast of the island of years, its volcanoes have again broken forth, and FOG Bouro, in the Molucco group, 7 m. N of the embouchure of the Diri.

FOGGIA, a considerable town of Naples, the cap. of the Capitanata. Pop. in 1832, 20,687. It has gates, but no walls, and the houses are irregularly scattered about in the outskirts; but the streets are wide and clean, the shops are large and well-supplied, and the whole has an air of opulence and prosperity. F. long possessed importance, from being a staple for wool and corn, and the seat of the dogano or office for collecting a tax on the sheep which pass periodically to and from the pastures of Puglia. In former ages, when intestine wars were frequent, and property insecure, it was necessary to collect rents and other dues at a central establishment of this nature; and the office of F. was even authorized to appoint deputations to other towns: of late, however, this has been comparatively disused. The square of F., and several of its streets, are undermined with vaults, where corn—the produce of the fertile La Puglia— is stored and preserved from year to year, the sides of them being faced with stone, and their mouth covered with boards over which the soil is slightly thrown. A great fair—chiefly for wheat and wool—is held here in May.—The foundation of F. dates from the 9th cent. It is supposed to have sprung from the ruins of Arpi or Argyrippa, situated 4 m. distant, of which some faint vestiges are yet extant.
