F. was the residence of Frederic II.; and his wife, Isabella of England, died here. It suffered severely from an earthquake in 1731. FOGGIA, PHOGIA, or FOILLERI, a small town and port of Asiatic Turkey, on the E side of the entrance to the gulf of Smyrna, 3 leagues E by S of Sahib island. The port is 2 m. in length, and 1 in breadth, and is finely sheltered on all sides but the N. The town is situated on a tongue of land projecting from the SE side of the port. Pop. 2,000. Oil and wine are the chief exports. With the exception of a small cultivated valley surrounding the town, the whole adjacent country appears deserted, mountainous, and rocky without any depth of soil; but the rocky ridges covered with wild flowers, dwarf firs, and Valonia oaks, present a green and picturesque appearance.
FOGGY (CAPE), a cape on an island near the S coast of Alashka, in N lat. 56° 31'.—Also a cape on the W coast of America, in N lat. 54° 54'.
FOGGY ISLAND, an island in the N. Pacific ocean, near the W coast of America, in N lat. 56° 10'. It is about 9 leagues in compass.

FOGLIA, a river of Italy, which rises in the Apennines, 4 m. W of Sestino in Tuscany, and, flowing from W to E, falls into the gulf of Venice at Pesaro, after a course of 40 m. It was the Pisaurus of the ancients.
FOGLIZZO, a small town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. of Turin, 5 m. NW of Chivasso, on the l. bank of the Orca. Pop. 1,500.
FOGLOE, the most easterly of the Faroe islands, in N lat. 62° 3'.
FOGNANO, a small town of Italy, in the Romagna of Tuscany, on the Lamone, 16 m. WSW of Faenza, and 21 m. W by S of Forli.
FOGO, a parish in the Merse, Berwickshire, 3 m. ENE of Greenlaw, intersected by the Blackadder. Pop. in 1811, 450; in 1841, 455.

FOGO, a small island near the E coast of Newfoundland, opposite Gander bay, in N lat. 49° 40'.- Also one of the Cape de Verde islands, lying W of Santiago, in N lat. 14° 50', W long. 24° 34'. It is entirely volcanic in formation; and rises in its central peak to an alt. of 9,157 ft. Its circumf. is about 40 m. Pop. in 1843, 7,000. After a silence of 50 torrents of burning lava issuing suddenly from its 7 craters caused considerable devastation in 1848 .- Also an island in the Mozambique channel, near the embouchure of the Quizumgo.
FOHENAGH, a parish in co. Galway, 34 m. SW of Ahascragh. Area 8,685 acres. Pop. in 1841, 2,004.

FOHR, a small island of Denmark, on the W coast of the duchy of Sleswig, in N lat. 54° 43'. It is about 12 m. in circumf., and contains an area of 25 sq. m. It is administratively divided betwixt Jutland and Sleswig. Pop. 4,750. It has a harbour capable of containing 40 or 50 merchantmen. Its chief exports are wheat, brandy, butter, and lard.
FOHRAG, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, 18 m. S of Yezd.
FOHREJ, or FOHRAJ, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Meckran, 260 m. WNW of Kej.
FOI-DE-REYROLIERES (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Garonne, cant. of Saint Lys. Pop. 1,293.
FOISSAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Aspieres. Pop. 1,899.
FOISSIAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Montreval. Pop. 2,262.
FOISSY, a village of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Villeneuve, 11 m. E of Sens.

FOIX, an arrondissement, canton, and town of France, in the dep. of Ariege.-The arrond. comprises the 8 cant. of Ax, La Bastide-de-Seron, Les Cabanes, F., Lavalenet, Querigut, Tarascon-sur- Ariege, and Vic-Dessos. Area 189,584 hect. Pop. in 1841, 92,300; in 1846, 94,451.—The cant., comprising 26 com., had a pop. of 21,669 in 1841.—The town is situated on the l. bank of the Ariège, 48 m. SSE of Toulouse, in N lat. 42° 57′ 47". Pop. in 1841, 4,980. It has some trade in iron ware, cattle, leather, and wool.—The adjoining country, called the co. of Foix, borders on Languedoc, and is in general hilly. It has mines of iron, extensive woods, and occasionally good pasturage; and is now comprised in the dep. of Ariege and the valley of Andorra.
FOJANO, a town of Tuscany, on the Chiana, 15 m. SSW of Arezzo. Pop. 6,357.—Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of Molise, 22 m. SE of Campobasso. Pop. 2,000.

FOK, a district of Nigritia, in Bertat, extending between the E bank of the Tumat and Abyssinia. It is generally mountainous, and is intersected by several torrents. FO-KIEN, FUH-KIEN, FOU-KIAN, OF FO-KEEN, a province on the SE coast of China, between the parallels of 23° 35' and 28° 47', and from 0° 22' to 4° E long. of Pekin; bounded on the N by the prov. of Che-kiang; on the E by the strait of Formosa and the sea of Corea; on the S by Quang-tung; and on the W and NW by Kiang-si. Its N and NW borders are defined by the high range of Nan-ling. The line of coast is generally bold, and bordered with islands. This prov. is of large extent, having an area of 53,480 sq. m., and comprehending the islands of Hae-mun and Hae-tan, the Pang-hao or Piscadore islands, and the island of Formosa. The surface is generally mountainous. Its rivers chiefly rise on the W skirts of the prov., and flow SE. The principal are the Min-ho; the Si-ho, a branch of the Min-ho; and the Tshang, which flows into the large bay of Amoy. The Min, or Min-ho, drains all the country E of the Wu-hi hills. Quicksilver, copper, lead, and iron, are produced; and it is conjectured that there are goldmines, but none are allowed to be worked. The mountains are covered with trees suitable for shipbuilding; abundance of rice is cultivated, and agriculture in several districts is skilfully prosecuted. The high grounds are disposed in terraces rising FOL above each other, to which water is conducted in pipes made of bamboo. On these terraces, in the N part of the prov., the tea plant thrives to perfection. When the soil is too sandy to admit of rice cultivation, they plant the sweet potato; barley and wheat are also grown. Orange, lemon, and mulberry trees are abundant. In this prov. are manufactured steel implements used in all the arts, cloth from hemp and cotton, and paper and pencils. The silks and cloth are of extraordinary fineness and beauty. The principal exports are bohea or black tea, camphor, sugar, china ware, and green cloth. The port of Enfouy was formerly open to European vessels, but all their trade has been transferred to Canton. Considerable commerce is carried on between F. and Japan, Formosa, the Philippine islands, Java, Cambodia, and Siam. The trading-craft is numerous, and the sailors of this prov. are the best seamen in China. Fu-chufu, the cap., is a most important commercial city. Amoy, Ya-kow, Chin-chew, Hing-hwa-fu, Fu-ningchu, Tan-shwuy, and Ke-lung, are also important emporiums. The pop. of the prov. is computed at nearly 15,000,000; but the census of 1762, as given by De Guignes, estimated it at that period at only 7,643,035. See Amoy and Fu-chu-fu.
FOKLISTOV, an island on the E coast of Asiatic Russia, in the sea of Okhotsk, opposite the embouchure of the Ouda, to the NW of Shantarskie. It is 30 m. in length, and 6 m. in breadth.
FOKSHANI. See FOCZANI.
FOLCHI, a river of Nigritia, in the kingdom of Haussa, flowing from S to N, and joining the Kuarama on the l. bank.
FOLDEN-FIORD, a large bay of Norway, in the prov. of Drontheim, in N lat. 64° 42'. It is dangerous to navigate.
FOLDVAR (Duna), a town of Hungary, in the com. of Tolna, on the r. bank of the Danube, in N lat. 46° 48′ 25″. Pop. 10,400.
FOLEMBRAY, a village of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Coucy, 6 m. SE of Chauny, and 14 m. W by S of Laon. Pop. 1,057. It has an extensive bottle-work.

FOLESHILL, a town and parish in the co. of Warwick, 2 m. NE of Coventry. Area 2,810 acres. Pop. in 1831, 6,969; in 1841, 7,063. It participates largely in the silk and ribbon manufactures of Coventry, and has a considerable number of plain engine and jacquard engine looms.

FOLGE-FOLDENFJELD, a rocky peninsula of Norway, intersected by the parallel of 60° N; and bounded on the W, N, and E, by the Hardangerfiord. It extends above 35 m. from S by W to N by E, and its breadth varies from 15 to 20 m. On the SE it is connected with the mainland of S. Bergenhuus. It is composed of rocky masses rising on all sides with a steep acclivity to an alt. of 5,600 ft., and supporting a table-land, along the middle of which ranges a field of perpetual snow, 20 m. long, and about 10 m. in breadth, from which descend glaciers, which have occasionally supplied the ice-cellars of London.
FOLGIAS, a powerful people of Upper Guinea, in the interior from the Grain coast and Sierra Leone. They are now united to the Karows, but depend on the sovereign of Monou.
FOLGOAT (LE), a village of France, in the dep. of Finistère, 14 m. NNE of Brest.
FOLGOZINHO, a village of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, com. and 15 m. W of Guarda. Pop. 350.

FOLIGNO, a town of the Papal states, in the duchy of Spoleto, on the Topino, near its confluence with the Maroggia, 15 m. SE of Perugia. Pop. 9,000. It is an ancient town, and is supposed to have owed its extension to the influx of fugitives from the invasion of the Lombards in the 6th cent. It is a bishop's see, and has some good streets, without any public edifice of note. Here are manufactures of silk, paper, parchment, and woollens.
FOLK. See FELK.
FOLKE, a parish of Dorsetshire, 3 m. SSE of Sherborne. Area 1,260 acres. Pop. in 1831, 281; in 1841, 318.

FOLKINGHAM, a town and parish in the co. of Lincoln, 26 m. SSE of Lincoln. Area 1,700 acres. Pop. 820. In the neighbourhood are the ruins of a castle, destroyed by Oliver Cromwell, and also the remains of the monastery of Sempringham.
FOLKINGTON, a parish in Sussex, 44 m. SSW of Hailsham. Area 2,110 acres. Pop. 198.

FOLKSTONE, or FOLKESTONE, a scaport and parish in the hund. of Folkestone, co. of Kent, 15 m. SSE of Canterbury, and 70 m. ESE of London, on the line of the South-eastern or Dover railway, by which it is 83 m. from London, and 5 m. from Dover. Area of p. 4,360 acres. Pop. in 1801, 3,706; in 1831, 4,296; in 1841, 4,413. Area of township 680 acres. Pop. in 1801, 3,259; in 1831, 3,638; in 1841, 3,723. F. lies close upon the shore of the English channel, between Dover and Hythe, and opposite to Boulogne; in a hollow between two precipitous cliffs. It consists chiefly of three streets, rather irregularly built along the acclivities of the western cliff. The beach is favourable for bathing, and the town is much resorted to as a watering-place. The cliffs command fine sea-views, and in clear weather the French coast is distinctly visible.—The harbour of F. was constructed in 1809 by a joint-stock company. It is entirely artificial, and formed by rubble stone piers, enclosing an area of 14 acres. The spring-tides average about 18 to 20 ft., and neap-tides from 12 to 14 ft.; but the harbour is left dry at low water, and the greater part of the interior is blocked up by a bank of shingle rising to the height of several feet above high water, and leaving only a channel of inconsiderable width along the side of the main pier. Extensive improvements are now being made on the harbour, the principal of which is the construction of a pier or breakwater at the entrance to the present harbour. This pier is to be carried out to the extent of about 400 yds. beyond the present harbour, and whilst it will protect the harbour from being obstructed by shingle, it will form the W boundary of a harbour covering more than 100 acres, in which at low water of spring-tide there will be a depth of water averaging from 10 to 25 ft.—The position of this harbour enables vessels, with the prevailing SW winds, to cross to Boulogne, when the communication with Calais from the more eastern English harbours cannot be effected. It is also the most easily accessible of all the harbours on the same coast, which are almost all tidal harbours, dry, or nearly so, at low water, and formed by piers carried out from the mainland. The SE railway company purchased the harbour of F., with the view of establishing a steam-communication direct from this point to Boulogne, a distance of 27 m. There is now a regular tidal service between the two harbours twice each way in the 24 hours; and the growing importance of F. may be gathered from the fact that the customs' duties, which in 1847 amounted to £4,008, in 1848 amounted to £8,128; in 1849 to £42,260; and in the first 6 months of 1850 to £41,316.—By the reform act F. has been added to Hythe for parliamentary representation. On the heights there is now a strong battery, and the coast is additionally defended by 3 martello towers. The celebrated Dr. William Harvey, discoverer of the circulation of the blood, was a native of this place.

FOLKTON, a parish and township in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. SSE of Scarborough. Area of p., 5,040 acres. Pop. 580. Area of township, 2,540 acres. Pop. 251.
FOLLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Bessines, 22 m. E of Bellac, on the l. bank of the Gartempe. Pop. 1,658.
FOLLONICA, a village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 6 m. W of Gavorrano, on the Mediterranean. A large quantity of iron-ore from Elba is smelted here.
FOLLIFOOT, a township in the p. and 2 m. WNW of Spofforth, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,990 acres. Pop. 358.
FOLLISTOWN, or FONLYSTON, a parish in co. Meath, 21 m. SE of Navan. Area 653 acres. Pop. in 1831, 137; in 1841, 138.
